title: The Better Part
subtitle: Mary sits at the Lord's feet while Martha serves

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of your many tasks fall silent.
The works of the active life draw the mind into care and distraction;
sit down, like Mary, and rest.
The water flows down to the lowliness of the valley,
and runs off from the swellings of the hill.
gospel: And after these things, Jesus entered into a certain village,
into Bethany, which means the house of obedience.
And a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.
And she had a sister called Mary,
who, sitting at the Lord's feet, heard his word.
But Martha was busy about much serving;
Lord, hast thou no care that my sister hath left me alone to serve? Tell her therefore to help me.
And the Lord answering, said to her:
Martha, Martha, thou art careful, and art troubled about many things.
Mary hath chosen the best part, which shall not be taken away from her.
behold: The Lord came to the house of Martha and Mary.
He turned aside often to that house, and freely,
because of the special friendship and devotion of those who dwelt there.
Those sisters, loving Jesus with their whole heart,
received him with reverence and eagerness,
rejoicing at the coming of so great a guest.
Martha at once made ready to provide a meal for the Lord and his disciples,
and labored in her serving, that she might fittingly care for them.
But Mary placed herself at the feet of the Lord.
She, with eyes and ears intent upon him,
gazed upon his most beautiful face,
and was refreshed in his sweet speech,
and beyond what can be told she delighted in his look and his words.
but was intent only upon the sweetness of the divine word,
and from that rich table of the Lord she gathered crumbs of the word of the Lord.
Martha stood and was busy, that she might feed the Lord with bodily food;
Mary sat and listened, that she might be fed by her Lord with spiritual food.
consider: How much better the soul is than the body,
so much sweeter is the spiritual food in the mind than the carnal food in the belly.
As Augustine says: the more humbly she sat, the more deeply she received.
Martha, complaining of her sister, speaks from the person of those
who, still ignorant of divine contemplation, think only the works of love pleasing to God.
But Mary, who rested in the words of the Lord and was held by a wondrous sweetness,
at the cry of her sister, as if waking from sleep, grew afraid for her quiet;
and with her face bent down to the earth, she was silent,
and committed her cause, as one at rest, to the Judge,
not preparing a word to answer, lest she should slacken or break off the intent of her listening.
Martha, Martha — the doubling of the name is a sign of love.
Thou art careful, and art troubled about many things, and art occupied about many.
If therefore thou wouldst live in gladness, do not do many things;
for in many doings thou shalt be the less.
But one thing is necessary: to cleave unceasingly to God.
Mary hath chosen the best part, the safer and the worthier.
As if he said: thou hast not chosen the bad, but she the better.
Greater is he who reclines than he who serves.
Even now she has tasted beforehand that sweetness and joy of the homeland,
though not as she shall grasp it in time to come.
Martha's part is not blamed, for it too is good;
but Mary's part is praised, because it is the best.
It shall not be taken away from her, for she has chosen what will always remain.
In this life it begins and grows; in the other it shall be more fully perfected.
What now she sees through a glass in a dark manner, then she shall see face to face.
And the fire of love, which here begins to burn,
when she shall see the very one whom she loves, shall blaze more greatly into love.
For charity never falls away.
The Lord answered Martha on Mary's behalf;
and he who had been called upon as judge became her advocate.
He did not blame the work, but he distinguished the gift.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who for us camest in the flesh,
through the union of the divine nature and the human,
let the bowels of thy mercy be moved over me,
thine unworthy servant, a sinner and guilty.
By the faith, the merits, and the prayers of that Martha,
who received thee as a guest in her virginal womb,
and of that Mary who kept thy words, pondering them in her heart —
of that most merciful Mother of thine —
come into my mind through the infusion of grace:
that I may love nothing but thee,
seek nothing, and desire to think of nothing but thee;
but that thou alone mayest be to me my hope and my every work.
I pray that even here I may in some measure taste beforehand
the sweetness of thy consolation. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Intenta erat Martha quomodo pasceret Dominum; intenta erat Maria quomodo pasceretur a Domino.
latinJewel.english: Martha was intent on how she might feed the Lord; Mary was intent on how she might be fed by the Lord.
